attack_vector: The mathematical rationale and Lean code model the Turing Machine's communication using `variable (C : X → CrossSeq)` and `eval_Y : CrossSeq → Y → Bool`. This explicitly assumes that the crossing sequence `C(x)` is determined *exclusively* by the left-hand fragment `X` and acts as a one-way message sent to the right-hand side `Y`. Real Turing Machines compute using two-way communication: the head can cross back and forth across the boundary, meaning the actual crossing sequence is dynamically determined by both sides, `CS(x, y)`. By artificially restricting the mathematical model to one-way communication, you forbid any valid TM algorithm that interleaves reads from both sides of the cut (e.g., reading one bit of X, crossing, then reading a bit of Y). This completely violates Algorithmic Agnosticism.
director_pivot_advice: To correctly bound 2-way TM communication, you cannot define the crossing sequence as a function of just one side. You must define it over the joint input, `CS : X × Y → CrossSeq`. Then, you must formally prove the 'Cut-and-Paste' (or Splicing) lemma for Turing Machines: if a TM produces the same crossing sequence for inputs `(x1, y1)` and `(x2, y2)`, it will inevitably undergo the exact same crossing sequence for the hybrid input `(x1, y2)` and yield an identical output. Only by using the Cut-and-Paste lemma can you apply Fooling Sets to two-way algorithms without restricting their computational freedom.
